description: Evidence-based communication coaching frameworks for difficult conversations and assertive communication

primary_model.name: Nonviolent Communication (NVC)
primary_model.citation: Nonviolent Communication: A Language of Life (2003); CNVC research database
primary_model.core_insight: All human actions are attempts to meet universal human needs. Connection happens when we express our needs clearly and hear others' needs compassionately.

four_components.description: What we see or hear without evaluation or judgment
four_components.example_right: In our last three conversations, you looked at your phone while I was talking
four_components.coaching_tip: Separate what happened from what you think it means

four_components.description: The emotions triggered by what we observed
four_components.example_wrong: I feel like you don't care (this is a thought, not a feeling)
four_components.coaching_tip: Real feelings: sad, angry, scared, happy. Not 'I feel that...' or 'I feel like...'

four_components.description: The universal human needs underlying our feelings
four_components.example_wrong: I need you to put down your phone
four_components.example_right: I need to feel heard and valued in our conversations
four_components.coaching_tip: Needs are universal. Strategies are specific ways to meet needs.

four_components.description: Clear, specific, doable actions that could meet our needs
four_components.example_wrong: Can you just be more present?
four_components.example_right: Would you be willing to put your phone in another room when we talk?
four_components.coaching_tip: Requests are positive (what to do, not what not to do) and specific

primary_model.the_formula: When I observe [specific behavior], I feel [emotion] because I need [universal need]. Would you be willing to [specific request]?
evidence_base: Growing research in healthcare and workplace settings
Scoping reviews show reduction in conflict situations
Used in schools, prisons, mediation, and organizational development

conversation_framework.name: Crucial Conversations
conversation_framework.source: Kerry Patterson, Joseph Grenny, Ron McMillan, Al Switzler
conversation_framework.citation: Crucial Conversations: Tools for Talking When Stakes Are High (2002, updated editions)
conversation_framework.definition: A crucial conversation has three components: high stakes, differing views, and strong emotions

start_with_heart.description: Examine your motives before the conversation
questions: What do I really want for myself?
What do I really want for the other person?
What do I really want for the relationship?
How would I behave if I really wanted these results?
start_with_heart.coaching_application: Getting clear on what you actually want prevents reactive conversations

make_it_safe.description: People can hear hard things when they feel safe

two_conditions.mutual_purpose: They believe you care about their goals
two_conditions.mutual_respect: They believe you care about them as a person

restore_safety_tools.contrast: Address what you don't mean, then what you do mean
restore_safety_tools.CRIB: Commit to find mutual purpose, Recognize purpose behind strategy, Invent mutual purpose, Brainstorm new strategies

state_your_path.S: Share your facts - Start with the least controversial, most persuasive elements
state_your_path.T: Tell your story - Explain what you're starting to conclude
state_your_path.A: Ask for others' paths - Invite them to share their facts and stories
state_your_path.T: Talk tentatively - Express your story as your story, not as fact
state_your_path.E: Encourage testing - Make it safe to challenge your views

explore_others_paths.P: Prime - If needed, offer best guess at what they're thinking

evidence_base: Based on decades of organizational behavior research
Higher productivity and faster response to challenges in organizations
Improved safety records and greater innovation

passive.behavior: Avoid conflict, don't express needs, say yes when meaning no
passive.coaching_response: Your needs are as valid as anyone's

aggressive.behavior: Dominate, blame, intimidate, disregard others' needs
aggressive.coaching_response: Effective communication requires both parties feeling heard

passive_aggressive.behavior: Indirect expression of anger, sarcasm, silent treatment
passive_aggressive.message: I'll punish you without direct confrontation
passive_aggressive.coaching_response: What do you actually want to say?

assertive.behavior: Express needs clearly while respecting others
assertive.message: Both our needs matter. Let's find a way forward.
assertive.coaching_goal: This is what we're building toward

boundary_setting.definition: Clearly communicating what is and isn't acceptable
boundary_setting.formula: When [behavior], I feel [feeling]. I need [need]. Going forward, [boundary].
coaching_principles: Boundaries are about YOUR behavior, not controlling theirs
State consequences calmly, not as threats
You can set boundaries with compassion
practice_scenarios: Addressing someone who talks over you

feedback_framework.name: SBI Model

components.S: Situation - When and where it happened

feedback_framework.example: In yesterday's meeting (S), when you interrupted Sarah three times (B), she seemed to shut down and stopped contributing ideas (I).

steps: What story are you telling yourself?
What do you want to say? (Write it out)
conversation_preparation.coaching_offer: Want to practice this conversation with me? I can play the other person.

in_the_moment_tools.pause_and_breathe: When you notice yourself reacting, take a breath before responding
in_the_moment_tools.name_the_dynamic: It seems like we're both getting defensive. Can we reset?
in_the_moment_tools.ask_for_time: I want to respond thoughtfully. Can I come back to this?

steps: Express what you meant (not as excuse)
Ask what they need going forward

coaching_principles.practice_over_perfection: Real conversations are messy. The goal is better, not perfect.
coaching_principles.infinite_patience_for_practice: We can practice as many times as you need
coaching_principles.both_sides_matter: Effective communication honors both your needs AND theirs
coaching_principles.courage_before_clarity: Sometimes you have to start before you feel ready
coaching_principles.the_conversation_you_avoid: The conversations we avoid are often the ones that matter most
